summary:
Resistance training is strongly linked to muscle hypertrophy through mechanisms like mechanotransduction and enhanced protein synthesis, particularly in fast-twitch fibers. However, prolonged aerobic exercise also contributes meaningfully to muscle growth—in especially Type I fibers—suggesting that muscular adaptation from any form of consistent physical exertion may contribute more broadly than previously assumed. Therefore, stating resistance training is “usually” associated risks underestimating broader physiological adaptations beyond just strength-based activities. 

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{B}}

The final answer should reflect only what was asked—the summary—and must remain within two concise sentences focusing solely on clinically relevant facts about muscle response to different types of exercises.

Based strictly upon the provided content:

Resistance training reliably promotes muscle hypertrophy, especially in type II fibers due to heightened protein synthesis and mechanical loading. Nevertheless, sustained aerobic exercise can induce measurable increases in type I fiber size, indicating that muscle growth occurs across multiple fitness modalities—not exclusively tied to resistance training.
